content:
# 1 Peter Chapter 5 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), pages 268-269 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1PE_005_page_001.jpg, 1PE_005_page_002.jpg

### Counsel for Elders and the Whole Community

1. So I appeal to the elders among you, as a fellow elder myself and a witness of Christ's sufferings, and as one who will share in the glory that is about to be revealed:

2. Be shepherds of God's flock that is in your care. Watch over them — not because you have to, but because you want to, as God desires. Not for shameful profit, but with an eager heart.

3. Don't lord it over those entrusted to you. Instead, be examples that the flock can follow.

4. And when the Chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the crown of glory that will never fade away.

5. In the same way, you who are younger, place yourselves under the guidance of the elders. And all of you, clothe yourselves with humility toward one another, because God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.

### Trust God with Your Worries

6. So humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God, and in due time he will lift you up.

7. Turn all your worries over to him, because he cares about you deeply.

8. Stay alert and keep watch! Your enemy the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, looking for someone to devour.

9. Stand firm against him, solid in your faith, knowing that your fellow believers throughout the world are enduring the same kinds of suffering.

10. And after you have suffered for a little while, the God of all grace — who called you into his eternal glory through Christ — will himself restore you, strengthen you, establish you, and set you on a firm foundation.

11. To him belongs all power forever and ever. Amen.

### Final Greetings

12. I have written you this brief letter with the help of Silvanus, whom I regard as a faithful brother. My purpose has been to encourage you and to testify that this is the true grace of God. Stand firm in it.

13. The church in Babylon, chosen together with you, sends you greetings. So does my son Mark.

14. Greet one another with a kiss of love. Peace to all of you who are in Christ.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verses 1-4 — The shepherd metaphor:** Peter addresses the elders (presbyteroi) not from above but from alongside — he is a "fellow elder" (sympresbyteros). This is remarkable. The man who walked with Jesus, who saw the Transfiguration, who denied him three times and was restored, calls himself simply a co-elder. The threefold contrast in verse 2-3 is striking: not by compulsion but willingly, not for greed but eagerly, not as domineering overlords but as examples. Every abuse of spiritual authority violates all three.

**Verse 4 — The unfading crown:** The "crown of glory" (ton tes doxes amarantinon stephanon) uses amarantinos — "unfading," a word that evokes the amaranth flower, which never wilts. Greek athletic victors received wreaths of celery or laurel that would brown and crumble within days. Peter promises something permanent. The Sinaiticus text is clear here, and the imagery bridges the Greco-Roman world of athletic competition with the eschatological hope of the church.

**Verse 5 — Clothe yourselves in humility:** The verb enkombosasthe comes from enkomboma, a work apron tied over clothing — the garment of a servant. Some scholars hear an echo of John 13, where Jesus tied a towel around himself and washed the disciples' feet. Peter was there. The quotation is from Proverbs 3:34 (LXX): God opposes the arrogant but gives grace to the lowly. James 4:6 quotes the same verse. Humility is not thinking less of yourself; it is thinking of yourself less.

**Verse 7 — Cast your anxiety on him:** The verb epiripsantes ("having cast") recalls Psalm 55:22 (LXX 54:23): "Cast your burden on the Lord." Peter transforms theological instruction into pastoral care. The reason is not "because he can handle it" but "because he cares about you" (hoti auto melei peri hymon). God is not merely powerful. He is attentive.

**Verse 8 — The roaring lion:** The image is vivid and would resonate with communities who knew that Christians were, in fact, being fed to lions. The devil is not presented as a tempter here (as in the Gospels) but as a predator. The command to be "sober" (nepsate) and "watchful" (gregoresate) echoes Jesus's words in Gethsemane — the very place where Peter fell asleep.

**Verse 10 — Four verbs of restoration:** After suffering comes restoration: katartisei (mend, restore — the word used for mending fishing nets in Mark 1:19), sterixei (strengthen, make stable), sthenosei (give inner strength), themelioosei (lay a foundation). The Sinaiticus text includes all four verbs. Suffering is real but temporary; God's restoration is comprehensive.

**Verse 12 — Silvanus the secretary:** "Through Silvanus" (dia Silouanou) likely means Silvanus served as the letter-carrier and perhaps the amanuensis (secretary). This is probably the same Silas who traveled with Paul (Acts 15-18). Peter calls him "the faithful brother" — high praise in one adjective. The phrase "this is the true grace of God" summarizes the entire letter: the grace you are experiencing in your suffering is genuine. Do not doubt it.

**Verse 13 — Babylon:** "She who is in Babylon, chosen together with you" — the feminine article likely refers to the church (ekklesia is feminine in Greek). "Babylon" is almost certainly a code name for Rome, as it is in Revelation. After the destruction of Jerusalem's temple in 70 CE, "Babylon" became the standard Jewish and Christian cipher for the imperial capital. "Mark my son" is traditionally identified as John Mark, the author of the Gospel of Mark, whom Peter here claims as a spiritual son.

**Verse 14 — Kiss of love:** Peter's closing is distinctive. Paul typically says "holy kiss" (philemati hagio); Peter says "kiss of love" (philemati agapes). This small difference carries Peter's pastoral warmth. The letter that began with suffering ends with peace — eirene, the Greek equivalent of the Hebrew shalom. It is not the absence of trouble but the presence of God in the midst of it.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
